org_constraint: Include the keyword "mentor" in the story.
new_constraint: Avoid using the keyword "mentor" in the story.

    (Drafting the story)
    Mr. Thorne didn't just teach history; he taught resilience.
    In the back row sat Elias, a boy whose shoulders were always hunched as if bracing for a blow. Elias had a brilliant mind for dates and battles, but his voice never rose above a whisper. He was terrified of being wrong.
    One Tuesday, Mr. Thorne handed out a blank sheet of paper. "Write a letter to a historical figure who faced a challenge similar to yours," he said.
    Elias froze. "I don't know what to write."
    Mr. Thorne walked over, his presence steady and grounding. "You don't have to be perfect, Elias. You just have to be honest."
    Over the next few weeks, Mr. Thorne became a constant source of encouragement. He didn't just grade papers; he sat with Elias during lunch, listening to his fears about his family's financial struggles and his own insecurities. He showed Elias that every great leader was once a person who felt small.
    He guided the boy through the process of finding his own strength. He was a steady hand in the storm of Elias's anxiety.
    By the end of the semester, Elias stood at the front of the room. His voice trembled at first, but then it grew firm. He spoke of courage, of the weight of expectations, and of the power of finding someone who believes in you when you can't believe in yourself.
    Mr. Thorne watched from the back, a small smile on his face. He hadn't just taught a lesson; he had helped a boy find his footing.

    *Story:*
    The classroom was always filled with the scent of old paper and sharpened pencils. Mr. Gable was a man of few words, but his eyes held a keen understanding of the students who sat before him. He didn't just see grades; he saw the hurdles each child had to jump over to get there.

    In the third row sat Sarah. She was a brilliant artist, but her hands shook whenever she had to present her work. Her home life was a whirlwind of noise and instability, and she often felt like she was drifting without an anchor.

    One afternoon, Mr. Gable stayed late. He sat across from Sarah, looking at a charcoal sketch of a bird with broken wings.

    "You have a gift, Sarah," he said softly. "But you're letting your fear of the world's judgment keep your hands from moving."

    He didn't just give her advice; he gave her a path. He began to set small, manageable goals for her. First, she had to share her sketches with just one person. Then, she had to describe her process to the class. He was there for every step, offering a steady hand and a quiet word of affirmation when her confidence wavered.

    He became her guide, the person who saw her potential when she only saw her obstacles. He taught her that courage wasn't the absence of fear, but the decision that something else was more important.

    Months later, the school held an art gallery. Sarah stood next to her centerpiece—a vibrant, soaring phoenix. When a judge asked her about the inspiration, Sarah didn't look at the floor. She looked at the crowd, her voice clear and steady.

    "It's about rising," she said. "Even when you think you're grounded by your circumstances."

    In the back of the room, Mr. Gable watched her. He didn't need a title to know what he had done. He had helped a girl find her wings.
